metadata.title: All paths at once, and only the classical one survives

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Hola, Liam — in for Bear. Why don't they? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.segment: All paths at once, and only

beats.narration_text: A particle travels from here to there. Classical physics says it takes one path. Quantum mechanics says it takes all of them — simultaneously.

card.headline:
All paths at once,
and only the classical one survives

beats.narration_text: If a particle really does travel every possible path, why does it appear to follow just the one path that minimizes the action? All other paths should show up. Why don't they?

card.headline:
All paths taken. One path seen.
Why does only the classical path survive?

beats.narration_text: Each path contributes a phasor — a unit arrow in the complex plane — whose angle is the action along that path divided by hbar. Actions differ path to path, so each phasor points in a different direction.

For paths far from the classical one, the action changes rapidly — a small wiggle shifts the phase enormously. The phasors for these wildly different paths point every which way and cancel each other out. Their sum is nearly zero.

But near the classical path, something special happens. The action is stationary there — meaning small wiggles barely change it. The phasors for all these nearby paths nearly agree in direction. They add constructively, producing a large resultant.

The classical path is exactly the one where the action is stationary — where its first derivative is zero. Paths clustered near it all carry nearly the same phase, so their phasors line up. The quantum amplitude is dominated by this region of constructive interference.

The principle of least action is not a postulate imposed on nature. It emerges from destructive interference. Every path exists. The classical one is just the one that survives, because it is where the quantum phases conspire to add rather than cancel.

This is Feynman's sum-over-paths formulation of quantum mechanics. In the limit where hbar approaches zero, the classical region narrows to a knife-edge, and the quantum particle traces the single classical trajectory — mechanics recovered from interference.

props.headline: This is Feynman's sum-over-paths formulation of quantum mechanics. In the limit…

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: imagine five paths between two points. Off-paths A and E have actions differing by pi from each other — their phasors point opposite and cancel. Paths B and D flank the classical path C; their phases nearly match C's. Add them all: A and E cancel, B and D reinforce C. The resultant points along C. The classical path dominates.

Every path contributes. Off-paths cancel by destructive interference. Near-classical paths add constructively. The classical trajectory is the region of stationary phase — where quantum mechanics hands you back Newton.

card.headline:
Stationary action = constructive interference.
All other paths cancel.
Classical physics emerges.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The principle of least action is not a postulate imposed on nature. It emerges from destructive interference. Every path exists. The classical one is just the one that survives, because it is where the quantum phases conspire to add rather than cancel. This is Feynman's sum-over-paths formulation of quantum mechanics. In the limit where hbar approaches zero, the classical region narrows to a knife-edge, and the quantum particle traces the single classical trajectory — mechanics recovered from interference.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [All paths at once, and only the classical one survives] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
